meta.name: DBAS-16 Dysfunctional Beliefs About Sleep
meta.fullName: Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep Scale-16
meta.description: The DBAS-16 is a 16-item self-report measure that assesses sleep-related cognitions, including beliefs, attitudes, expectations, and attributions about sleep. Dysfunctional beliefs about sleep play a key role in perpetuating insomnia and are a primary target of cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I).
meta.author: Morin et al. (2007)
meta.reference: Morin CM, Vallieres A, Ivers H. Dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (DBAS): Validation of a brief version (DBAS-16). Sleep. 2007;30(11):1547-1554.
meta.instruction: Several statements reflecting people's beliefs and attitudes about sleep are listed below. Please indicate to what extent you personally agree or disagree with each statement by selecting a number from 0 (strongly disagree) to 10 (strongly agree).

questions.text: I need 8 hours of sleep to feel refreshed and function well during the day
When I don't get the proper amount of sleep on a given night, I need to catch up on the next day by napping or on the next night by sleeping longer
I am concerned that chronic insomnia may have serious consequences on my physical health
I am worried that I may lose control over my abilities to sleep
After a poor night's sleep, I know that it will interfere with my daily activities on the next day
In order to be alert and function well during the day, I believe I would be better off taking a sleeping pill rather than having a poor night's sleep
When I feel irritable, depressed, or anxious during the day, it is mostly because I did not sleep well the night before
When I sleep poorly on one night, I know it will disturb my sleep schedule for the whole week
Without an adequate night's sleep, I can hardly function the next day
I can't ever predict whether I'll have a good or poor night's sleep
I have little ability to manage the negative consequences of disturbed sleep
When I feel tired, have no energy, or just seem not to function well during the day, it is generally because I did not sleep well the night before
I believe insomnia is essentially the result of a chemical imbalance
I feel insomnia is ruining my ability to enjoy life and prevents me from doing what I want
Medication is probably the only solution to sleeplessness
I avoid or cancel obligations (social, family) after a poor night's sleep

ranges.label: Low Dysfunctional Beliefs
ranges.description: Your score indicates few dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep. You generally have realistic expectations and attitudes regarding sleep. This indicates that your sleep quality and patterns are in good condition, allowing you to get adequate rest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy and realistic attitudes about sleep
Keep practicing good sleep hygiene habits
Stay informed about evidence-based strategies for good sleep
Continue maintaining your good sleep habits
Keep a consistent sleep schedule

ranges.label: Mild Dysfunctional Beliefs
ranges.description: Your score indicates some dysfunctional beliefs about sleep. Certain unrealistic expectations or worry about sleep may be contributing to sleep difficulties.
suggestions: Learn about the normal variability of sleep and sleep needs
Challenge beliefs that one poor night of sleep will ruin the next day
Avoid excessive monitoring of your sleep patterns
Consider reading about cognitive behavioral approaches to sleep
Establish fixed bedtime and wake-up times

ranges.label: Moderate Dysfunctional Beliefs
ranges.description: Your score indicates a moderate level of dysfunctional beliefs about sleep. These beliefs may be perpetuating sleep difficulties and increasing sleep-related anxiety.
suggestions: Consider cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I)
Work on challenging catastrophic thoughts about the consequences of poor sleep
Reduce reliance on sleep medication as the primary solution
Practice relaxation techniques before bed
Discuss your sleep concerns with a healthcare provider

ranges.label: High Dysfunctional Beliefs
ranges.description: Your score indicates a high level of dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep. Strong maladaptive cognitions about sleep are likely contributing significantly to insomnia and sleep-related distress.
suggestions: Strongly recommended to seek CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia)
A sleep specialist can help restructure unhelpful beliefs about sleep
Challenge the belief that medication is the only solution for sleep problems
Learn that sleep is a natural process that cannot be forced — reducing effort often helps
Address catastrophic thinking about the daytime consequences of poor sleep
